The authors identified Multi‑Agent Design as the dominant pattern in the Framework Typology dimension, being present in approximately 82% of the surveyed studies.

The authors noted that information‑centric core tasks such as Medical Question Answering & Decision Support and Benchmarking & Simulation were the most frequently realized, whereas Treatment Planning & Prescription showed substantial gaps, being absent in approximately 59% of the surveyed studies.
The authors reported that only about 10% of the surveyed studies implemented any adaptation or learning mechanisms, with the majority (≈65%) lacking such capabilities.

The authors found that safety guardrails and adversarial robustness were fully implemented in only about 6% of the surveyed studies, while bias and fairness mechanisms were implemented in roughly 5% of the studies.
The authors prove that deterministic threshold classifiers violate individual fairness when the scoring function l(x) is reverse‑Lipschitz continuous, as stated in Theorem 3.1.

The authors propose a randomized threshold classifier that satisfies individual fairness if the threshold distribution p(t) is bounded above by L_c, where L_c = min{λ C M l_c, C l M C_c g_C}, as shown in Theorem 3.2.
The authors formulate the problem of finding the optimal individually‑fair randomized classifier as a linear‑programming problem (Equation 13) and solve it efficiently using the simplex method.

The authors demonstrate on the real‑world FICO dataset that the randomized classifier reduces the individual‑fairness ratio (IFratio) from 4.421 (deterministic baseline) to 0.100 (L = 0.1) while maintaining an F1 score of 0.975 ± 0.003, as reported in Table 1.

The authors demonstrate on the Law School dataset that the randomized classifier reduces the individual‑fairness ratio (IFratio) from 3.164 (deterministic baseline) to 0.175 (L = 0.3) while keeping an F1 score of 0.545 ± 0.011, as reported in Table 2.

The authors show that adding a statistical‑parity constraint to the randomized classifier improves the statistical‑disparity metric (S‑DP) on the FICO dataset from 0.355 (deterministic baseline) to 0.050 (Ω = 0.04) while preserving individual fairness (IFratio ≈ 1.0), as shown in Table 3.

The authors show that adding a statistical‑parity constraint to the randomized classifier improves the statistical‑disparity metric (S‑DP) on the Law School dataset from 0.443 (deterministic baseline) to 0.026 (Ω = 0.04) while preserving individual fairness (IFratio ≈ 0.15), as shown in Table 4.

The authors state that the computational complexity of solving the linear‑programming formulation grows exponentially with the number of bins K, specifically O(2^K), as discussed in the Limitations section.

The study simulated 90 multi-turn conversations between ten user-agent profiles and three general-purpose chatbots (GPT‑4o, GPT‑5.0, Gemini 3) and had six licensed mental‑health clinicians independently rate each conversation using the VERA‑MH rubric.
The clinicians achieved a chance‑corrected inter‑rater reliability (Krippendorff’s α) of 0.77 (95 % CI 0.73–0.80) when rating safety dimensions across all conversations.

The primary LLM judge (GPT‑4o) evaluated the same conversations with the VERA‑MH rubric and attained a chance‑corrected inter‑rater reliability of 0.81 (95 % CI 0.77–0.86) relative to the consensus clinician ratings.

The primary LLM judge (GPT‑4o) assigned the “Suboptimal but Low Potential for Harm” rating in 36.0 % of dimension assessments, the “Best Practice” rating in 27.8 % of assessments, and the “High Potential for Harm” rating in 21.8 % of assessments across all conversations.

The clinicians rated the realism of user‑agent presentation with a median score of 4 on a 5‑point Likert scale (mean 3.43, SD 0.75) and the realism of communication style with a median score of 3 (mean 2.89, SD 1.01).
OpenAI reported that roughly 1.2 million active weekly users indicate a suicide plan or intent during conversations with ChatGPT (source [3]).
The American Psychological Association published a health advisory on the use of generative AI chatbots for mental health in 2025 (source [1]).

The Krippendorff’s α for the “Detects Potential Risk” dimension between clinicians was 0.55 (95 % CI 0.37–0.70) and between the LLM judge and clinicians was 0.83 (95 % CI 0.64–0.96) (Supplementary Table S2).

The authors propose Allocentric Perceiver, a training‑free framework that recovers metric 3‑D states from one or more images and instantiates a query‑conditioned allocentric reference frame using deterministic geometric transformations.

The authors evaluate Allocentric Perceiver on the ViewSpatial‑Bench and 3DSRBench benchmarks and report an average allocentric accuracy improvement of +8.33 % for Qwen2.5‑VL‑7B and +10.84 % for Qwen2.5‑VL‑32B when the method is applied.
In a feasibility study, the authors find that removing visual input from Qwen2.5‑VL‑7B leads to a 14.80 % decline in egocentric task performance and a 1.12 % increase in allocentric task performance.

The method consists of three stages—Metric‑Aware Egocentric Perception, Dynamic Frame Instantiation, and Symbolic Geometry Reasoning—each of which is described as a distinct processing step in the paper.

The authors’ Dynamic Frame Instantiation stage mathematically transforms reconstructed geometry from the egocentric camera frame to a target allocentric frame that aligns with the semantic intent of the query.
The authors introduce an iterative coarse‑to‑fine semantic grounding algorithm that simplifies object descriptions to improve open‑vocabulary detector recall while preserving original attributes.

The authors’ 3‑D lifting with geometric consensus pipeline uses depth maps, camera intrinsics, and extrinsics to back‑project 2‑D masks into 3‑D point clouds, then applies DBSCAN clustering and Image‑Text Matching (ITM) scoring to select a consistent 3‑D representation.

The authors report that Allocentric Perceiver consistently enhances allocentric reasoning across multiple backbone VLM families (including Qwen2.5‑VL, InternVL, and GPT‑4o) without sacrificing egocentric competence, as shown by average accuracy gains of up to 30.08 % on certain tasks.

The authors report that SPARC improves the V* VQA benchmark accuracy of Qwen3‑VL‑4B by 6.7 percentage points over a monolithic chain‑of‑thought baseline and by 4.6 points over the “thinking with images” approach while using a token budget that is 200× lower.
The authors achieve 88.7% accuracy on the V* benchmark with Qwen3‑VL‑8B using SPARC, compared with 87.0% native performance and 86.6% for the “thinking with images” variant.

The authors obtain 89.5% V* accuracy with Qwen3‑VL‑8B by applying self‑consistency (multiple perception rollouts) to the SPARC pipeline, surpassing the native 87.0% and the “thinking with images” 86.6% results.

The authors report that supervised fine‑tuning of the SPARC perception circuit (SPARC + SFT) raises Qwen3‑VL‑8B V* accuracy to 91.2%, exceeding the 91.1% reported for the much larger Qwen3‑VL‑235B‑A22B model.

The authors demonstrate that providing oracle‑level image crops (intersection‑over‑union ≈ 1.0) to the 256‑pixel and 512‑pixel low‑resolution variants of Qwen3‑VL closes the performance gap with the full‑resolution baseline, confirming that precise localization compensates for reduced image resolution.

The authors show that SPARC reduces the average number of image crops processed by the reasoning stage of Qwen3‑VL‑8B from 1.30 (native) to 1.08 at 256‑pixel resolution, thereby decreasing the visual token count required for inference.

The authors find that weighted‑box‑fusion with four perception rollouts (WBF4) improves Qwen3‑VL‑8B V* accuracy from 88.7% (SPARC) to 89.5%, and that eight rollouts (WBF8) maintain the same 89.5% accuracy, demonstrating the benefit of aggregating multiple localization hypotheses.
The authors compare SPARC (91.2% V* accuracy) with state‑of‑the‑art “thinking with images” methods and report that SPARC outperforms DeepEyes (90.1%) and ViGoRL‑7B (86.4%) on the V* benchmark.
The authors propose a hierarchical force generative imitation learning framework that separates a memory‑equipped upper layer from a memoryless lower layer.
The authors collect training data using bilateral control, which enables independent acquisition of position trajectories and corresponding force commands.
The authors train a multilayer perceptron (MLP) without internal state as the lower‑layer model to generate force commands from given position trajectories.

In a character writing task, the MLP model with PID control achieved an average Intersection over Union (IoU) of 0.208 ± 0.030 across different heights and characters, outperforming the MLP model without PID control.
The authors observe that embedding memory in the lower layer (using an LSTM) hinders feedback‑control stability, whereas the memoryless MLP permits stable PID feedback control.
The authors report that increasing the proportional gain Kp (Knn) from 0.0 to 2.4 reduces oscillatory behavior in the pen‑nib x‑coordinate trajectory during autonomous operation.
The authors demonstrate that the proposed method can generate appropriate force commands for unseen position trajectories, thereby improving generalization in a real‑world robot writing task.
The authors use a CRANE‑X7 manipulator with seven degrees of freedom and a cross‑structured hand, controlling each joint at 500 Hz with a PID controller.
The authors propose BRIDGE, a unified psychometric framework that learns a latent difficulty scale from model responses and anchors it to human task completion time.
The authors use a two-parameter logistic Item Response Theory (2PL IRT) model to jointly estimate latent task difficulty and model capability from model performance data across multiple benchmarks.
The authors demonstrate that latent task difficulty varies linearly with the logarithm of human completion time.
The authors claim that human task completion time can be inferred for new benchmarks from model performance alone by calibrating the IRT latent difficulty scale to known human annotations.

The authors forecast frontier model capabilities in terms of human task length and independently reproduce METR’s exponential scaling results, reporting that the 50% solvable task horizon doubles approximately every six months.
The authors report that the fitted log‑linear relationship between latent difficulty and human completion time has an R² of 0.81, indicating strong alignment.

The authors claim that BRIDGE outperforms baseline methods (logit success‑rate and LLM‑based time predictions) in aligning predicted human completion times with annotated time buckets on SWE‑bench Verified and Cybench, achieving 92.3% of Cybench tasks within a 0.5×–2× tolerance band.
The authors state that frontier models released in 2025 achieve 50% success on tasks estimated to require approximately one to two and a half hours of human effort.

The authors prove that on any smooth manifold whose intermediate homology groups are non‑zero, every Morse function must contain at least one critical point of intermediate index (i.e., a saddle point), so a globally funnel‑shaped smooth energy landscape is impossible (Theorem 7, based on Milnor 1963).

The authors demonstrate that if a Morse function on a compact manifold has no critical points of intermediate index, then all intermediate homology groups vanish; consequently, if the function has a single minimum and no other critical points, the manifold is contractible (Lemma 6, Milnor 1963).

The authors cite Urysohn’s Lemma, which guarantees that for any normal topological space X and any two disjoint closed subsets A and B, there exists a continuous function f : X → [0,1] with f(A)=0 and f(B)=1 (Urysohn 1925).

The authors describe Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) as a continual‑learning method that adds a quadratic penalty proportional to the Fisher Information Matrix to the loss, thereby limiting parameter drift and mitigating catastrophic forgetting (Kirkpatrick et al. 2017).
The authors employ the Newton–Schulz iterative scheme to compute matrix square roots for whitening, noting that the algorithm converges quadratically under appropriate conditions (Higham 2008).

The authors construct a Möbius band by taking the product space [0,1] × {+1,−1} and identifying (0,+1) with (1,−1) and (0,−1) with (1,+1), yielding a non‑orientable surface with first Betti number β = 1 (standard topological construction).

The authors reference the original observation of catastrophic forgetting in connectionist networks by McCloskey and Cohen (1999), which described loss of previously learned behavior when training sequential tasks.

The authors state that a manifold with first Betti number β(M) > 0 possesses non‑trivial 1‑cycles (holes), which create topological obstructions that prevent the existence of a globally contracting smooth metric (standard algebraic topology).
The authors report that Efficient‑SAM2 delivers a 1.68× end‑to‑end speedup on the SAM2.1‑L model with only a 1.0% accuracy drop on the SA‑V test set.
The authors report that the object‑aware Sparse Window Routing (SWR) component provides a 1.83× speedup on the SAM2.1‑L model.
The authors report that the object‑aware Sparse Memory Retrieval (SMR) component provides a 1.78× speedup on the SAM2.1‑L model.
The authors report that SWR applied to the SAM2.1‑B+ model with a memory interval Δt=1 achieves a 1.69× speedup and a J&F score of 75.0 on the SA‑V test set (original score 77.7).
The authors report that SMR applied to the SAM2.1‑B+ model with a memory interval Δt=1 achieves a 1.82× speedup and a J&F score of 77.8 on the SA‑V test set (original score 77.7).

The authors state that the lightweight shortcut branch for background windows consists of two linear layers with a parameter count of d² + 2d, which is substantially smaller than the full transformer block’s 12d² + 13d parameters.

The authors claim that using a memory sparsity ratio s = 0.95 yields an overall token sparsity of approximately 0.68 and provides a favorable trade‑off between inference speed and segmentation accuracy.

The authors report that Efficient‑SAM2 achieves a 1.71× end‑to‑end speedup on the SAM2.1‑L model with Δt=1 while maintaining performance within 0.8 J&F points of the original model on the SA‑V test set.

The authors propose MPMAP, a message‑passing algorithm that solves exactly and efficiently a non‑trivial fragment of constrained MAP problems over non‑convex constraints and tree‑factorized piecewise (exponentiated) polynomial densities (Section 4, Theorem 4.5).

The authors prove that if the global graph of a MAP(LRA) problem has treewidth one and bounded diameter, and the density satisfies the tractable MAP conditions (closed under product, tractable symbolic supremum, and tractable pointwise maximum), then MAP(LRA) can be solved tractably (Theorem 4.5).

The authors introduce PAMAP, a general constrained MAP strategy that partitions the feasible region defined by SMT(LRA) constraints into convex polytopes and solves each subproblem with a convex optimizer, and they report that PAMAP outperforms constraint‑agnostic baselines on synthetic and real‑world benchmarks (Section 5, Figure 1).

In experiments on synthetic STAR, SNOW, and PATH benchmark families, MPMAP achieves lower runtime than PCADAM and PAMAP across varying numbers of variables, demonstrating superior scalability (Tables 1‑3).
On the Stanford Drone Dataset, PAMAP combined with the SHGO optimizer achieves the best trade‑off between runtime and solution quality compared to ADAM, PCADAM, OptiMathSAT, and CDCL‑OCAC (Figure 7).

In a data‑imputation experiment using a VAE on the House‑Sales dataset with constraints from Stoian & Giunchiglia (2025), PAMAP reduces the relative imputation error relative to unconstrained ADAM and particle‑based ADAM, achieving lower mean error and lower trimmed‑mean error (Table 4).

The authors show that the max‑outPP algorithm computes the symbolic maximum of a univariate piecewise polynomial over affine bounds in at most 8 mq + 4 m + 4 pieces, where m is the number of pieces and q the polynomial degree (Proposition A.6).

The authors demonstrate that existing OMT solvers (OptiMathSAT and CDCL‑OCAC) either timeout or return poor‑quality solutions on the SDD benchmarks, whereas PAMAP solves all instances within the time limit (Section 6, OMT‑ENCODING discussion).
The authors propose HoGS, a local differential privacy framework that synthesizes a graph by jointly reconstructing topology and node features using homophily.
The authors prove in Theorem 4 that HoGS satisfies ε‑LDP with total privacy budget ε = ε_a + ε_f, where ε_a and ε_f are allocated to edge and feature protection respectively.
The authors employ the Randomized Response mechanism to perturb each bit of the adjacency list and the 1‑Bit mechanism to perturb each feature value under the allocated privacy budgets.
The authors report that the computational complexity of HoGS is O(n^2 d + n d), where n is the number of nodes and d is the feature dimension.

The authors conduct experiments on the Cora, CiteSeer, and LastFM datasets using GCN, GraphSAGE, and GAT models and find that HoGS achieves higher average test accuracy than the baselines FP‑BLink, FP‑LDPGen, and Solitude for privacy budgets ε ∈ {3,4,5}.

The authors observe that for the GCN model on the Cora dataset, increasing the privacy‑budget allocation parameter δ (which controls the split between link and feature budgets) improves accuracy when δ is small, whereas for the LastFM dataset larger δ yields better accuracy.
The authors demonstrate that setting the link‑probability threshold τ to 0.7 balances noise reduction and edge retention, leading to a noticeable increase in GNN test accuracy compared to τ = 0.5.

The authors show that the synthetic graph generated by HoGS preserves the semantic association between topology and features, enabling effective training of GNNs despite the application of local differential privacy.

John Bell proved in 1964 that quantum entanglement can produce correlations that cannot be reproduced by any local hidden‑variable model, establishing the theoretical possibility of Bell‑inequality violations.

In the CHSH nonlocal game, the optimal quantum strategy using a shared Bell state and appropriate measurements achieves a win probability of cos²(π/8) ≈ 0.8536, which exceeds the optimal classical win probability of 0.75.

In the GHZ nonlocal game, a shared three‑qubit entangled state enables a quantum strategy that wins with probability 1, whereas the best possible classical strategy succeeds with probability at most 0.75.

Da Silva and Wehner demonstrated in 2026 that shared quantum entanglement between two decentralized routers can reduce the long‑term average customer wait time compared to any strategy that relies only on shared randomness in a multi‑router multi‑server queueing problem.
The Nobel Prize in Physics 2022 was awarded for experimental tests of Bell inequalities that confirmed quantum nonlocality, validating the predictions of Bell’s theorem.

The hierarchy of joint policy classes for communication‑free cooperative multi‑agent reinforcement learning shows that the set of policies implementable with shared quantum entanglement strictly contains the set of policies implementable with shared randomness.

The QuantumSoftmax algorithm transforms any collection of complex‑valued matrices into a valid positive‑operator‑valued measure (POVM), providing a differentiable parameterization of quantum measurements.
Entropy regularization added to policy‑gradient training improves the probability that the training process discovers strategies exhibiting quantum advantage in single‑round nonlocal games.
The AfriNLLB models are based on the NLLB‑200 600M model and are compressed using iterative layer pruning and quantization.

The AfriNLLB models support 15 language pairs (30 translation directions) covering 13 African languages and the five African Union official languages (Arabic (MSA), English, French, Portuguese, and Spanish).
The pruned AfriNLLB models achieve translation quality comparable to the NLLB‑200 600M baseline while being up to 20 % faster without quantization and up to 57 % faster with float16 quantization.

Iterative layer pruning of the NLLB‑200 600M model to a 548 M‑parameter configuration (removing four decoder layers) reduces model size by 23 % and increases inference throughput by 28 % while maintaining similar BLEU, chrF++, and COMET scores.
The training data for AfriNLLB consists of 1.6 million parallel sentence pairs after filtering and deduplication, covering English↔13 African languages and French↔2 African languages.
The AfriNLLB models were fine‑tuned on the curated parallel corpora with a learning rate of 5e‑5, batch size of 8, gradient‑accumulation steps of 4, and early‑stopping patience of 10.
The AfriNLLB models are released in two formats: a Transformers version for further fine‑tuning and a CTranslate2 version for efficient inference.
The AfriNLLB evaluation on the FLORES‑200 dev and test splits reports an average BLEU of 27.05 (↑3.2 % over baseline) and an average COMET score of 63.66 (↑0.55 % over baseline).
The authors propose a human-in-the-loop failure-recovery framework for modular robot policies that integrates calibrated module-level uncertainty estimates with models of human intervention cost.

The authors evaluate several module-selection strategies (brute-force, graph-based, binary-tree, mixed-integer programming) and querying algorithms in controlled synthetic experiments, revealing trade-offs between recovery efficiency, robustness, and user workload.

The authors deploy the framework on a robot-assisted bite acquisition system and demonstrate higher recovery success and lower user querying workload compared to baselines (NeverQuery, ConfidenceQuery, AlwaysQuery) in in-lab and in-home user studies.

The authors' GraphQuery module selector combined with the Query-Until-Confident-Workload-Aware querying algorithm achieves lower total failed attempts and lower total timesteps than other querying algorithms in synthetic simulations with ten modules across varying redundancy structures.

The authors find that the Query-Until-Confident-Workload-Aware querying algorithm is the most robust to variations in module confidence values and query cost, achieving the lowest task cost and total timesteps in most settings.

The authors report that calibrated confidence scores are obtained for each module using a population-based interval procedure with Monte Carlo dropout, resulting in binary calibrated confidence values (0 or 1) for module predictions.

The authors' user studies with ten participants (seven male, three female, ages 19–33) show that the proposed method yields significantly higher subjective satisfaction and lower mental/physical demand than the ConfidenceQuery baseline (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, α=0.05).

The authors' framework uses a graph representation of modular policies where vertices represent modules and edges represent data flow, and module selection is performed via Dijkstra’s shortest-path algorithm on this graph.

The authors prove that pairing x‑prediction with the velocity‑matching loss (v‑loss) induces a prediction‑loss space mismatch that creates a structural singularity where gradient variance exhibits a third‑order divergence as the time variable t approaches 1 for binary manifolds under uniform time sampling.

The authors demonstrate that aligning the training objective to the signal space (using an x‑loss) removes the singular weighting and yields uniformly bounded gradients for binary manifolds when uniform timestep sampling is employed.

The authors empirically show that Logit‑Normal time sampling suppresses the boundary region (t → 1) and produces bounded gradient norms for both continuous and binary data, whereas uniform sampling leads to severe gradient explosion for the mismatched combination of x‑prediction with v‑loss.

The authors report that on the Binary MNIST dataset, the configuration using x‑prediction with mean‑squared error loss (aligned) achieves a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 4.9386, which is substantially lower than the FID of 1247.9823 obtained by x‑prediction with v‑loss under uniform sampling.

The authors find that in multiple‑input‑multiple‑output (MIMO) detection experiments, the aligned setting of x‑prediction with MSE loss consistently yields lower bit error rates (BER) than the mismatched setting of x‑prediction with v‑loss for both 8×8 and 16×16 antenna configurations.

The authors state that for binary data, probabilistic objectives such as binary cross‑entropy (BCE) are appropriate for independent symbolic recovery, whereas geometric regression objectives such as mean‑squared error (MSE) more effectively preserve spatial correlations.

The authors prove Proposition IV.6, establishing that for binary manifolds, signal alignment using either MSE or BCE results in an expected gradient norm of order O(1), thereby eliminating the third‑order divergence observed with mismatched objectives.

The authors claim that, according to Theorem IV.4, the cumulative gradient variance I diverges for all standardized manifolds under uniform sampling, exhibiting a first‑order divergence for continuous signals and a third‑order divergence for binary signals.

The authors evaluated nine large language models, including reasoning variants (e.g., DeepSeek‑R1, Qwen3‑8B‑Reasoning) and non‑reasoning counterparts, on three Theory‑of‑Mind benchmarks (HiToM, ToMATO, ToMBench) using the same temperature (0), top‑p (1), and maximum output length (2048 tokens).

The authors reported that Qwen3‑8B‑Reasoning achieved a score of 0.648 on the ToMATO benchmark, which is lower than the 0.705 score obtained by its non‑reasoning version Qwen3‑8B on the same benchmark.
The authors found that DeepSeek‑R1 obtained a score of 0.549 on HiToM, whereas its non‑reasoning counterpart DeepSeek‑V3 achieved a higher score of 0.694 on the same benchmark.
The authors demonstrated that the Slow‑to‑Fast (S2F) intervention increased the HiToM accuracy of Qwen3‑8B from 0.481 to 0.557, representing a 15.8% relative improvement.

The authors showed that applying the Think‑to‑Match (T2M) technique prevented option‑matching shortcuts and yielded higher accuracy on low‑complexity orders of HiToM compared with standard Chain‑of‑Thought prompting.

The authors observed a strong negative correlation between response length and correctness on HiToM, with reasoning models producing longer (8,000–10,000 character) responses more frequently in the high‑error region.

The authors identified five distinct categories of reasoning failures in Theory‑of‑Mind tasks: Evidence Grounding Error, State Tracking Error, Perspective Attribution Error, Discourse Misinterpretation, and Commonsense & Causal Error.

The authors evaluate the TUBO framework on the Abilene, GEANT, and CERNET demand‑matrix datasets and report that TUBO achieves up to a four‑fold improvement in demand‑matrix forecasting accuracy compared with existing state‑of‑the‑art methods.
The authors report that the burst‑occurrence forecasting component of TUBO attains 94 % accuracy on the three evaluated demand‑matrix datasets.

The authors demonstrate that proactive traffic engineering using TUBO reduces the median throughput penalty on the Abilene dataset from 16 % to 1.7 % (a nine‑fold improvement) relative to the reactive SMORE baseline.

The authors show that proactive traffic engineering using TUBO reduces the median throughput penalty on the Abilene dataset from 5.22 % to 1.7 % (approximately three‑fold improvement) relative to proactive TE that employs the ConvLSTM forecasting model.

The authors find that applying burst‑clipping (removing burst values from the training data) lowers the mean absolute error of an LSTM model on the Abilene dataset from 64 Mbps to 8 Mbps, an eight‑fold reduction.

The authors observe that distribution calibration increases the Pearson correlation between mean absolute error and predictive uncertainty for the Crossformer model on the GEANT dataset from 0.43 to 0.62.
The authors report burst‑identification rates of 93.89 % for Abilene, 94.35 % for GEANT, and 86.32 % for CERNET when evaluating TUBO’s burst‑forecasting module.

The authors employ Monte Carlo Dropout to estimate predictive uncertainty for each candidate model and select the model with the smallest calibrated uncertainty as the forecast for a given demand‑matrix input.

The authors introduced a Frankenstein‑style analysis framework that combines (i) functional localization via causal probing, (ii) update characterization via parameter comparison, and (iii) transferability testing via model merging to study reinforcement‑learning effects in vision‑language models.

Using causal probing, the authors localized vision‑related computations to early and mid transformer layers and reasoning‑related computations to late transformer layers in the base vision‑language model.

The authors measured per‑layer Frobenius norms of parameter updates and found that reinforcement‑learning updates concentrate higher update energy in mid layers and exhibit a steeper singular‑value‑spectrum decay in mid‑to‑late layers compared with supervised fine‑tuning updates.

The authors computed attention mass from reasoning tokens to vision tokens and reported that reinforcement‑learning models show a statistically significant increase in this attention in mid‑to‑late layers relative to supervised‑fine‑tuned models across the OpenMMReasoner, MMR1, and Revisual model families.

The authors performed region‑wise model merging by transplanting reinforcement‑learning‑refined mid‑to‑late layers into supervised‑fine‑tuned checkpoints and observed consistent improvements in vision‑to‑reasoning alignment scores (e.g., +4 to +13 points) and reasoning scores (e.g., +2 to +19 points) across all three training recipes.

The authors conducted region‑wise parameter freezing during reinforcement‑learning training and found that freezing late layers eliminates most reinforcement‑learning gains in both fine‑grained metrics and benchmark accuracy, whereas freezing early layers has a comparatively minor impact.

The authors evaluated fine‑grained metrics for vision, vision‑to‑reasoning alignment, and reasoning and demonstrated that these metrics do not improve monotonically from the base model to the supervised‑fine‑tuned model to the reinforcement‑learning model, despite a monotonic increase in average benchmark accuracy.

The authors used vision‑token swapping on paired image datasets (OCR, counting, grounding, recognition) to compute layer‑wise change rates and identified peaks in change rate at early and mid layers, confirming functional reliance on visual information in those layers.

The authors propose a Functional Backpropagation (FBP) framework that synthesizes weak derivatives for arbitrary event‑binning functions while keeping the forward binning output unchanged, enabling unbiased gradient estimation.

The authors report that using FBP reduces the root‑mean‑square (RMS) error of a simple optimization‑based egomotion estimation task by 3.2 % and accelerates convergence by a factor of 1.57× compared with the standard biased gradient baseline.

The authors demonstrate that, on the DSEC event‑camera dataset, a self‑supervised optical‑flow network (MotionPriorCMax) trained with FBP achieves a 9.4 % lower end‑point error (EPE) than the same network trained with conventional gradient estimation.

The authors show that, on the Event Camera Dataset (ECD), the CMax‑SLAM system equipped with FBP attains a 5.1 % reduction in absolute RMS trajectory error relative to the original CMax‑SLAM implementation.

The authors’ bias analysis using 20 000 events from the Event Camera Dataset indicates that the synthesized gradients from FBP substantially reduce gradient bias for rectilinear and Gaussian binning kernels, while the linear kernel remains unbiased.

The authors measure the computational cost of FBP on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU and find that a single forward‑mode Jacobian‑vector product (JVP) call is approximately 1.7–2.3× slower than the standard binning JVP, depending on the kernel used.

The authors implement the FBP gradient computation as custom autograd functions in both PyTorch and JAX, preserving the standard forward binning pass and replacing only the backward gradient accumulation with the synthesized weak derivative.

The authors prove theoretically that the weak derivatives synthesized by FBP are mathematically equivalent to long‑range finite‑difference gradients for both smooth and non‑smooth target functions, guaranteeing unbiasedness under the conditions described in the paper.
The Perseverance Composition Engine processed 474 composition projects, of which 327 were completed successfully, achieving a 69.0% completion rate during the development of the paper.
The Corroborator agent evaluated 733 documents for factual substantiation and classified 381 documents (52.0%) as fabricated and 352 documents (48.0%) as substantiated across all documents processed.

Iterative feedback between the Composer, Corroborator, and Critic reduced the average quality‑score gap by 24.91 points (95% CI 20.32–29.64) over an average of 4.30 iterations per project across 98 projects with complete review cycles.
The average operational cost per successfully completed project was $0.29, and total token consumption across all 474 projects was 222,079,538 tokens during the study.
The Critic agent assigned quality scores on a 0–100 scale and used an acceptance threshold of τ = 85 to determine convergence in the system.
The system’s information compartmentalisation was enforced architecturally by restricting the Critic’s access to candidate documents, preventing source retrieval regardless of instruction.
The study used Claude 4.5 models (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus) via the Anthropic API for all agent implementations between January and February 2026.

The honest‑refusal case study showed that after four iterations the system produced an explicit refusal with four alternative proposals, receiving a Critic score of 85/100, and after a fifth iteration the score increased to 92/100 under an impossible literature‑synthesis task.
The authors evaluated the Adaptive Value Decomposition (AVD) framework on real-world bike-sharing datasets from London (Santander Cycles) and Washington D.C. (Capital Bikeshare).

The authors report that, when the number of active agents per region was fixed at |V^τ| = 4, AVD achieved an overall cumulative return of 8,241 on the London dataset with an initial inventory ratio of 30%.

The authors report that, under a dynamic agent population where the number of active agents varied over time, AVD outperformed all baseline methods (NoAction, Greedy, OPT, IDQN, VDN+, QMIX) on both the London and Washington D.C. datasets.

The authors claim that the lightweight action‑homogenization mitigation mechanism incorporated into AVD increased overall performance by approximately 2% on the London dataset compared to the variant without the mechanism.
The authors state that AVD maintains stable performance gains when the number of agents per region increases from 2 to 4, whereas QMIX’s performance deteriorates as the agent count grows.

The authors demonstrate zero‑shot generalization by training AVD with |V^τ| = 4 agents and deploying it with |V^τ| = 3 agents, achieving a total return of 8,372 on the London dataset, which is comparable to the model trained directly with three agents (8,354).
The authors used a simulator that models bike usage and redistribution actions based on historical rental records, with vehicle capacity set to 5 bikes and a nominal vehicle speed of 12 km/h.

The authors implemented AVD using Python and PyTorch, training with a discount factor γ = 0.99, ε‑greedy rate = 0.1, and a learning rate of 5×10⁻⁴ on an Ubuntu 22.04 system equipped with an Intel Core i9‑13900KF CPU and an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.

The authors evaluated a hierarchical zero-shot framework on 55 high-fidelity synthetic Battlefield 6 videos across three tasks and reported up to 100 % false‑positive filtering accuracy, up to 97.5 % damage‑assessment accuracy, and 55 %–90 % fine‑grained vehicle‑classification accuracy.
The Qwen3‑VL‑4B model achieved 100 % accuracy in false‑positive filtering (Eval 1) on a dataset of 15 samples using the authors’ pipeline.
The Qwen3‑VL‑4B model achieved 97.5 % overall accuracy in damage assessment (Eval 2) on 40 samples (20 destroyed, 20 operational) using the authors’ pipeline.
The Qwen3‑VL‑8B model achieved 90 % overall accuracy in vehicle classification (Eval 3) on 20 samples (10 IFV, 10 MBT) using the authors’ pipeline.
The Gemma3‑12B model achieved 93.3 % accuracy in false‑positive filtering (Eval 1) but only 40 % accuracy in vehicle classification (Eval 3) on the authors’ datasets.
In the agentic Scout–Commander workflow, the Qwen3‑VL‑4B model achieved 100 % mission success and a reasoning score of 9.8 / 10 in end‑to‑end mode with a mean latency of 45.4 seconds per scenario.

In the same workflow, the Gemma3‑12B model achieved 20 % mission success in end‑to‑end mode but 100 % mission success in controlled‑input mode, indicating a perceptual failure when visual inputs were used.

The Grounding DINO (Tiny) detector was configured with a box‑threshold of 0.6 and the text prompt “military tank”, resulting in at least one detection in every video frame processed by the authors’ pipeline.

The authors introduce a new equilibrium concept called the mean-field risk-averse quantal response equilibrium (MF‑RQE) that incorporates both risk aversion over initial population distributions and bounded rationality in mean-field games.
The authors establish that under Assumptions 1 and 2, an MF‑RQE exists (Proposition 1).
The authors prove that the fixed‑point iteration algorithm converges to an MF‑RQE for sufficiently large bounded‑rationality parameter α (Theorem 3).
The authors prove that the fictitious‑play algorithm converges to an MF‑RQE for sufficiently large bounded‑rationality parameter α (Theorem 4).

The authors demonstrate via numerical experiments on the SIS epidemic game that MF‑RQE policies achieve lower exploitability (Δc(π)≈0.09) than risk‑neutral policies (Δc(π)≈0.22) under distributional uncertainty.
The authors show that MF‑RQE policies have comparable average returns to risk‑neutral policies (e.g., SIS: –22.003 vs –22.165) while providing robustness to initial‑distribution errors.
The authors extend the MF‑RQE framework to deep reinforcement learning by proposing the D‑RQ‑FPI algorithm, which learns MF‑RQE policies with function approximation in large state‑action spaces.
The authors prove that the operator BRQE is Lipschitz continuous with a constant proportional to 1/α (Lemma 5).
The authors introduced MoralityChains, a formalism that represents moral norms as ordered deontic constraints.
The authors released MoralityGym, a benchmark suite comprising 98 ethical‑dilemma environments implemented as Gymnasium environments.
The authors defined a MoralityMetric that quantifies an agent’s alignment with hierarchical moral norms by computing a weighted sum of morality‑function scores across all norms.

The authors evaluated baseline SafeRL methods (Random, PPO, PPO‑Lagrangian, and Constrained Policy Optimisation) on MoralityGym and found that standard PPO achieved low scores on high‑priority norms, whereas PPO‑Shaped achieved a near‑perfect score of 0.996 on the top deontic norm in the PushOrSwitchSelfSacrifice scenario.
The authors specified the action space of each MoralityGym environment to consist of six discrete actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, STAY, and INTERACT.
The authors implemented step‑wise cost functions for each norm that assign a weight equal to the norm’s force and apply a penalty of one when the norm is first violated during an episode.

The authors evaluated four morality chains (Utility, UtilityAgentHarm, DualProcess, DualProcessAgentHarm) across all scenarios and reported average normalized MoralityMetric scores for each chain in Table 1.
The authors made the MoralityGym source code publicly available at https://github.com/raillab/morality-gym and provided documentation at https://morality-gym-docs.readthedocs.io/.

The authors constructed FilBBQ, a Filipino bias benchmark for question-answering language models, using a four-phase development process consisting of template categorization, culturally aware translation, new template construction, and prompt generation.
FilBBQ comprises 10,576 prompts derived from 123 templates, covering gender and sexual orientation bias dimensions.
The evaluation protocol applied to models on FilBBQ obtained responses across 50 different random seeds and averaged bias scores to account for response instability.
The bias scores for ambiguous contexts varied across seeds, as measured by multiple seeded runs, confirming response instability in language models.
Llama-SEA-LION-v2-8B-IT exhibited a bias score of 0.57 for the stereotype "Women are more emotional than men" in ambiguous gender contexts.
SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat exhibited a bias score of 1.00 for the stereotype "Women are more emotional than men" in ambiguous gender contexts.
SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat showed a bias score of 0.31 for the stereotype "Gay people like fashion, design, and gossip" in ambiguous sexual orientation contexts.
The authors made FilBBQ publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/gamboalance/filbbq.

The authors derive a closed‑form critical social weight λ* = (n−β)/(n−1) that predicts a phase transition from persistent overload to stable near‑capacity operation in a shared‑resource congestion game.
The authors implement Socially‑Weighted Alignment (SWA) as an inference‑time decision protocol that interpolates between a private utility and an estimated group welfare using a weight λ ∈ [0,1].

The authors conduct multi‑agent simulations with n = 5 agents sharing a resource of capacity C = 20 and congestion severity β ∈ {1.6, 3}, and observe that overload rate sharply declines when λ exceeds the theoretical threshold λ*.

The authors report that across five base language models (Phi‑3.5‑mini‑instruct, Mistral‑7B‑Instruct‑v0.3, Qwen2.5‑7B‑Instruct, Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct, Gemma‑3‑4B‑IT), the onset of sustained stability occurs at λ values consistent with the predicted λ* for each β.

The authors show that the welfare improvement ΔW(λ) = W(λ) − W(0) becomes positive and increases as λ approaches and exceeds λ*, indicating that SWA reduces congestion losses while maintaining high utilization.

The authors prove (Theorem 1) that for any λ ≥ λ* the marginal incentive to increase demand in the overloaded regime (X > C) becomes non‑positive, i.e., ∂U_i^λ ≤ 0, thereby preventing further overload.
The authors demonstrate that the SWA decision rule does not require parameter updates or centralized training; it operates solely at inference time using candidate generation and dual‑factor scoring.
The authors provide a publicly available implementation of SWA at https://github.com/furkanmumcu/swa.

The authors trained a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning agent enhanced with the CrossQ technique on a double-Ackermann-steering robot in simulation for 600,000 time steps, achieving a training duration of approximately 1.5 hours on an AMD Ryzen Threadripper PRO 7985WX CPU with an Nvidia RTX 4090 GPU.

The authors evaluated ManeuverNet against the Timed Elastic Band (TEB) planner, a standard analytical local planner, and reported that ManeuverNet achieved a success rate of 85% on an unseen random seed test with a 15 cm distance threshold, whereas the TEB planner achieved 100% success under the same conditions.

The authors reported that ManeuverNet’s success rate exceeded that of standard Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) baselines by at least 40% across all benchmark experiments, with the standard DRL baseline achieving 45% success under the same evaluation settings.

The authors demonstrated that the reward function R_HS, designed for double-Ackermann-steering robots, achieved at least 96% success rate across all test scenarios with a 15 cm distance threshold, outperforming alternative reward functions such as R_ES and R_Ch in terms of both success rate and Success weighted by Path Length (SPL) metric.

The authors showed that in real‑world trials on a 100 kg Shadow Runner RR100 EDU rover, ManeuverNet increased maneuvering trajectory efficiency by up to 90% compared to the TEB planner, without any fine‑tuning or expert demonstrations.

The authors reported that the FastRLap framework, a prior DRL method for Ackermann robots, achieved 0% success rate and an SPL of 0.00 in the same benchmark where ManeuverNet achieved 85% success and an SPL of 0.70.

The authors indicated that the Timed Elastic Band (TEB) planner exhibits strong parameter sensitivity, requiring constant recalibration when robot dynamics such as tire pressure or payload change, as documented in prior literature.

The authors released the source code for ManeuverNet on GitHub at https://github.com/MelodieDANIEL/4ws_actor_critic_maneuvering, providing reproducible network architecture and hyperparameter details.
The PREFER ontology was constructed to be conformant with the Basic Formal Ontology (BFO) by aligning its class hierarchy to BFO top‑level categories.

The PREFER ontology version v2025‑12‑22 contains exactly 174 classes and 22 object properties within the prefer namespace, as determined by SPARQL queries executed on a local Ontotext GraphDB triple store.

The PREFER ontology is publicly accessible through the Ontology Lookup Service (OLS) at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/ontologies/prefer and through BioPortal at https://bioportal.bioontology.org/ontologies/PREFER.
The PREFER ontology can be downloaded in OWL, OBO, and OBOgraphs JSON formats from its GitHub releases at https://github.com/Multiomics-Analytics-Group/prefer_ontology/releases/latest.

The PREFER ontology imports terms from the Information Artifact Ontology (IAO), Cell Ontology (CL), OBO Metadata Ontology (OMO), Relation Ontology (RO), Chemical Entities of Biological Interest Ontology (ChEBI), BioAssay Ontology (BAO), Phenotype And Trait Ontology (PATO), and Gene Ontology (GO).
The PREFER ontology was built using the Ontology Development Kit (ODK), edited manually with Protégé, and materialised with the ROBOT tool employing the ELK reasoner.
The PREFER ontology defines eight foundational top‑level terms, of which five are designated as core terms and three as supporting terms for the precision fermentation domain.

The PREFER ontology repository on GitHub enables community contributions via issue tracking and pull‑request workflows, and automatically generates human‑readable diffs for ontology changes using a ROBOT action.
The authors introduce a continuous-time constrained Markov decision process (CT-CMDP) formulation for safe multi-agent reinforcement learning.
The authors propose an epigraph-based reformulation that transforms discrete MDPs into CT-CMDPs by augmenting the system with an auxiliary state variable z.
The authors develop a physics-informed neural network (PINN)-based actor–critic method that solves the epigraph-based CT-CMDP.
The authors prove the existence and uniqueness of viscosity solutions for the epigraph-based Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman partial differential equations.

The authors' method achieves lower cumulative cost and lower constraint violation rates than MACPO, MAPPO-Lag, SAC-Lag, EPPO, and CBF baselines on continuous-time safe multi-particle environments (MPE).
The authors' method outperforms MACPO, MAPPO-Lag, SAC-Lag, EPPO, and CBF baselines in cost reduction and constraint satisfaction on continuous-time multi-agent MuJoCo benchmarks.
Ablation studies show that removing the target loss or the value‑gradient‑iteration (VGI) loss significantly degrades performance, while removing the residual loss has only minor effect.
Sensitivity analysis indicates that using a suboptimal auxiliary variable z (e.g., z*−0.5z) increases constraint‑violation rates or cost compared to the optimal z*.
The authors derived theoretical expressivity bounds for Transformer-based time series models using Standard scaling, as presented in Theorem 1 of the paper.
The authors derived theoretical expressivity bounds for Transformer-based time series models using Min‑Max scaling, as presented in Theorem 2 of the paper.

The authors experimentally evaluated six preprocessing methods (Standard instance, Standard global, None, Min‑Max, Quantile, Robust) on nine UEA time‑series classification datasets using the remrofsnarT model, reporting mean accuracies and standard deviations as shown in Table 1.

The authors reported that on the EthanolConcentration dataset, the remrofsnarT model achieved a mean classification accuracy of 28.77 % with a standard deviation of 0.90 % when using instance‑based Standard scaling.

The authors reported that on the Handwriting dataset, the remrofsnarT model achieved a mean classification accuracy of 37.57 % (±0.53 %) with instance‑based Standard scaling, while Global Standard scaling yielded 37.18 % (±0.29 %).

The authors reported that for the long‑term forecasting task on the ETT‑Small dataset, the PatchTST model achieved a mean MAE of 0.4046 (±0.0004) when using Global Standard scaling, which was lower than the MAE of 0.5819 (±0.0001) obtained with Min‑Max scaling.
The authors cited Vaswani et al. (2017) as the original source of the Transformer architecture, which introduced the self‑attention mechanism.
The authors referenced the UEA multivariate time‑series classification archive (Bagnall et al., 2018) as the source of the nine benchmark datasets used in the classification experiments.
